step.title: Step 12
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE shoots blue projectile upward')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 15, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE only works when player is close to the target (within ~2 rows). Target B frame is at rows 28-31, player at rows 37-39 - too far. Blue projectile from target C at rows 33-34 may block upward movement. Need to get closer to target B.
- Plan: Move UP to get closer to target B, then SPACE to shoot it.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 14 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player didn't move! Player is still at rows 37-39. The blue projectile at rows 33-34 is blocking upward movement. This is the same position as before the UP command.
So the blue projectile acts as a solid obstacle. I need to either:
1. Move the blue projectile out of the way
2. Find another way to destroy target B
Wait, let me reconsider the mechanic. The blue projectile from target C is following the player. When I move left/right, it moves with me. When I tried to move up, it blocked me.
Maybe I need to get rid of the blue projectile first? Or maybe I need to approach target B from a different angle?
Actually, let me think about this differently. The blue projectile is at rows 33-34, and the player is at rows 37-39. The gap is 3 rows. If I could move the projectile away, I could move up.
What if I move RIGHT first, then UP, then LEFT? The projectile might not block if I'm not directly below it.
Or maybe I should try SPACE again - maybe it shoots the projectile upward, clearing the path?
Let me try SPACE again to see if it shoots the blue projectile upward.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider what SPACE does. When I first used SPACE:
- Player was at rows 41-43, cols 32-35
- Target C frame was at rows 36-39, cols 32-35
- There was a white block at rows 40-40, cols 32-35 between player and target
- SPACE destroyed the frame and the white block merged with it
Now, when I used SPACE with target B:
- Player was at rows 37-39, cols 16-19
- Target B frame was at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
- Blue projectile was at rows 33-34, cols 17-18
- SPACE didn't destroy the frame
Maybe SPACE needs the player to be adjacent to the target frame (no gap). The first time, the white block was at row 40, just below the frame at row 39. So the player was effectively adjacent to the frame through the white block.
For target B, the player is at rows 37-39, and the frame is at rows 28-31. The gap is rows 32-36 (5 rows), with the blue projectile at rows 33-34.
So I need to get the player closer to the frame. But the blue projectile is blocking.
What if I move RIGHT to avoid the projectile, then UP, then LEFT back? Let me try that.
Actually, let me try a different approach. What if I move DOWN first, then RIGHT to go around, then UP to target B from a different column?
Wait, but SPACE shoots straight up. So I need to be directly below the target to shoot it.
Let me try SPACE again - maybe it shoots the blue projectile upward, which would clear the path.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player blocked from moving UP by blue projectile at rows 33-34. Player at rows 37-39, target B frame at rows 28-31. Need to clear the projectile or find another approach.
Plan: Try SPACE again to see if it shoots the blue projectile upward, clearing the path to target B.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try SPACE again - maybe it shoots the blue projectile upward
predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE shoots blue projectile upward')
action(['SPACE'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Blue: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Frame: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Frame: rows [24-27], cols [44-47]
Blue: rows [25-26], cols [45-46]
Frame: rows [28-31], cols [16-19]
Blue: rows [29-30], cols [17-18]
Blue: rows [33-34], cols [17-18]
Player: rows [37-39], cols [16-19]
Board changed: True
